content: Just as the 'reflexion' defined in high school English vocabulary acts as a core linguistic building block that lets students articulate metacognitive thinking about their own learning, the orthographic variant 'reflexion' (rather than the common 'reflection') can be framed as a deliberate, domain-specific primitive for LLM self-reflection mechanisms that anchors inconsistent self-correction perspectives to a grounded base. This orthographic distinction can be used to tag raw, unfiltered self-evaluations in LLM memory separate from polished final reflections, reducing the drift that occurs when multiple conflicting self-assessments get mixed in dynamic memory storage.
